commentBody: Thursday lite. The revealer was obvious; having the revealer (and the big boxes) made the puzzle much too easy (in my opinion, of course). This worked L(ikea)CHARM if you like your Thursday tricks on the very easy side. Nice idea, Joe Deeney, but I would have preferred normal size boxes for the stores.
I was surprised by the nonstandard plural SERAPHS.
I had [guess]timates for 12D, but of course, a SguessT isn't a thing
Cute puzzle, but awfully easy for a Thursday.  It would have been more of a challenge if the rebus squares had not been telegraphed.
Super cute. I loved this puzzle. Fun and cheeky without being obstinate. I needed harmonious noncombative company tonight- thank you!
Cute puzzle. One of the easier thursdays, but that’s not necessarily a bad thing. Enjoyable fast solve that didn’t have lots of obscure names.

I realize that the only qualification for the special entries is that they be stores that go into a (slightly) big(ger) box, but it would have been nice if all four were really BIG BOX STORES. LOWES and IKEA definitely qualify, but GAP is just a typical mall store, and ALDI is a moderately-sized limited-stock supermarket.

I applaud Mr. Deeney's cleverness, but frankly, he's ruined the Times puzzle for me. Like most people, I am sick of the endless bombardment of ads for big businesses. Now he has spoiled that refuge for me. Bah humbug. 
And why the anti-math comment?

I like the theme of this puzzle but ... entering the names as rebuses makes me think these are little box stores.

There's some symmetrical fun in the N and S: MAGMA spewing out of a CHASM and a short OBIT that reads NO ME.

Good puzzle today.  Clever theme and good rebus that wasn't sneaky about being a rebus.  Fun solve.

I had trouble in the northeast corner because first I had GUESSTIMATE but couldn't make GUESS work with the across clue.  Then I had TEE SLOTS and didn't know enough about MENORAHs to realize easily I was wrong.

Vivaldi asked for an amaretto at the local dive. He was disappointed.

Of all the yoga poses to try at the megaplex, downward dog really makes it hard to watch the movie.

Speaking of that, the model who was told to strike a pose one too many times opted for downward dog. It worked like a charm - coffee break instantly declared.

The contractor trying so hard to offer a low estimate for the project lost out because they were slowest to realize that there was no more American Flag Blue paint to be found anywhere.

Are we not giving IPA a “,briefly…” or “abbr.” anymore? Am I taking crazy pills? Am I an old man yelling at clouds?

Also I’ve never had a black or Belgian IPA, despite being pretty open to IPAs.
Right on TARGET!

Well, I finally found how to get to comments… Yay. Also yay, I figured out the big box stores theme early. I don’t agree with Gap or Aldi though. Yes to IKEA and Lowes. Made for a relatively easy but not fast Thursday puzzle

I admire the vision of constructors who come up with these sorts of ideas.  And I admire the word smithing that constructing such a beautiful thing.

But let me tell you what kind of shopper I am.  I want a long street, preferably like something out of Harry Potter, with teeny, tiny stores so picturesque you could just eat them up.  Each one specializes to the point that one can either gather it all in with one glance, or pore over it for hours.

In other words, I love the polar opposite of big box stores.  Every single one of them gives me a headache to even drive by.  And being in most of them makes my skin crawl.  And that made the puzzle slightly less appealing to me that it might have been.

I can't escape big box stores entirely--this is America, after all--but I avoid them to the best of my ability.
I don't think of GAP as a BIG BOX STORE, but I found this puzzle delightful!
Started with FA and filled in FAkEnewS. It took a while for me to discard that answer. Never heard of FANEDITS.

The BIG BOX STORE discussion just provoked a buried memory of an old episode of Taxi, in which Alex (Judd Hirsch) tries to explain to drug casualty Jim (Christopher Lloyd) that the blue berries he found weren’t blueberries.

hmmm. 
Mr. Maleska be rolling.

So easy.  So sadly, disappointingly easy.  Again.  Thursdays at the Times have become the opposite of what they were until quite recently - unpredictable and challenging and innovative and creative and surprising and engaging and fun.  This was... not that.

/boo to the Great Easening(TM)

They really changed things. You can’t pop the puzzle back up while you’re in the comments section. It doesn’t open the NYT app anymore.

Anyway, nice puzzle. The LOWES square had me for about eight minutes. Other than that, a nice bowl of popcorn.

Even with a year under the belt. Starting 1 across with a gimme. Either ACTI or ACTV basically made the puzzle fill itself. 

But I will say, for me, I spent maybe 30% of my solve trying to get my last cell. The NE themer. I just cold fry my brain to process it. Until I did. 

Interestingly. The note says don’t play in dark mode. Which I always do. Many times I can’t see circles when playing in dark mode. But these boxes were super clear. Interesting the editors put that note in there, which I saw after I was done. 

I liked the theme. But I didn’t. For me. And I  think for a lot of folks, the harder, the more enjoyable. I’m not here to boost my ego. I’m here to get my backside whooped (@$$ kicked) in a fair but punishing way.

I take issue with 12D, “Low Estimates” being clued as “Conservative Guesses”. There are plenty of situations where the conservative guess may in fact be intentionally on the high side, for example in budgeting for expenses.

Like Barry, I found it to be Thursday lite, but I enjoyed it nonetheless. 
Tiny bit of a religious theme going on, what with SERAPHS, MENORAH, EDEN and GRAVEN IMAGE in the grid.

Thanks, Joe.

I had airBOXSTORE, which I had never heard of before, but I get ads from stores selling online all the time (GAP and IKEA almost daily) and it seemed really cool.  Still does, even though I had to come to the column to find out why it wasn't working.

Thanks for a clever puzzle, Joe Deeney. When you have another I'll buy it.

commentBody: Harmless Thursday fun no doubt setited to provide an increasingly entrenched and unwelcome metric of maximum fun and minimal frustration.

commentBody:
Look! Look!

The Midi has a Thursday gimmick.

Is this a first?

The big, glowing boxes could just as well have had an arrow pointing to them, captioned with "There is a rebus here!!!". Why even have a rebus when discovering it is no challenge at all? Over here, VIVALDI'S Four Seasons is probably one of the few pieces of classical music almost everybody is familiar with, so it basically gave the theme to me for free.

Also, the themed squares were incorporated into words that had nothing to do with BIG BOX STORES - I always think there is something inelegant about a theme that does not meaningfully incorporate all the entries involved.

The fill was not very interesting, either.

Oh, and even though I am a huge beer snob, I've never seen a Belgian IPA. The concept seems very strange - Belgian beers are known for being malty rather than hoppy, and I've never had a bitter Belgian ale, so I don't really get how a Belgian style would show in an IPA.

I know this puzzle can be considered easy, but despite the lack of a greater challenge, I'm completely charmed by it. It took some thinking and figuring out, but not the hair-pulling kind. A Thursday Lite, I'd call it. It brought me memories of my stint working for the GAP in Austin, TX. Remember GAP greeters at the door? They'd just stand there at the entrance, blurting out "Welcome to the GAP!" My boyfriend at the time walked in one day and was greeted with a "Hello, how are you?" by this innocent kid who was on his first day at work. Big mistake. Huge mistake. My boyfriend, an extroverted jokester, stopped in his tracks and lovingly answered, "I'm so glad you asked. You really want to know? Let me tell you how my day's been going—" at which point I ran over and pulled my boyfriend away, apologizing to the wide-eyed kid and letting him know all was well and he was doing great. I'm still friends with that ex-boyfriend and 'til this day I tease him that it was because of him that the kid never came back for a second shift. And with that, I leave you with the fabulous funky The Gap Band and the song "You Dropped a Bomb on Me" - https://youtu.be/17lkdqoLt44?si=uKq1eacDvnaPOwAk Thank you, Mr. Deeney, for a very nice puzzle and the trip down memory lane!

commentBody:
Do I detect a sort of veiled apology for the ease of today's puzzle in Mr. McGowan's commentary?

"Thursday Puzzles 101 course" might be another term for "gut."

Could this be the start of a Wordplay-column revolution?

"In today’s puzzle, each of these four squares is a rebus"

I would pay enormous quantities of money to have this somehow revealed BEFORE I try to start solving the puzzle.

Does anyone else see the typo? It really confused me

Spoilers


39 down answers with Megalexes however if they are referring to what I think they are referring to it would be Megaplex, so the puzzle is missing the P.
In all my ~1500 puzzles, I have never noticed a NYT crossword using the same word twice in the grid (POSE) - I had assumed this was some kind of rule? Can anyone illuminate for me?
How does the rebus function work? I can't get more than one letter in those big boxes. Is it the pencil button? Because that didn't work either.

Finally! A rebus!

And then the NYT crossword editing team appeals to the lowest common denominator by highlighting exactly the squares in which to place them. This was a good puzzle that could have been made great by not highlighting the squares in which to place the rebi.
I am always intrigued by how it is decided what is a ‘tricky clue’.  They are invariably not the clues I struggle with. But Cli uses to be 151 - that’s completely inexplicable? Can anyone explain it?

You all want to talk about BIGBOXSTORES? Well, i want to talk about Shamash. 

I’ve never heard of a shamash, which is apparently the candle used to light all the other ones on a MENORAH. This is probably why i thought it said “stramash”, which is a Scottish word that basically means a brawl. So there i was, trying to work out if the seven letter answer could be “Glasgow” or “Scot pub” or somesuch. 

And when i finally looked up Shamash, Wikipedia did not illuminate me with Jewish candles but took me straight to an article about the ancient Mesopotamian sun god of that name, who was also known as Utu (in another confusing near-miss with one of this puzzle’s answers.) 

Finally on the right track, i am fascinated to see that MENORA derives from the ancient Proto-Semitic root -n-w-r, meaning "to shine" or "to give light," which is shared by other light-related words like the Arabic nar (fire) and minaret. Who knew??

I don't care what day it is as long as it's a fun puzzle, which this very much was, but a Thursday that's "approachable enough for solvers who are still getting their Thursday sea legs" - isn't that a Wednesday?
I've got a soft spot for a rebus (any puzzle that plays around with the letter entry to be honest) - I enjoyed this one as just a straight-up classic example of the genre. Plus a cute little visual element as well.
Good to see CORONA clued as a beer, too. Like "musk" should be clued as a scent...
Great job, thanks Joe!

As I solved this puzzle, I wondered how it would sit with our international solvers. Do they know these stores? Do they care to?

Aside from the “big box store” pun, I found the whole thing pretty uninspired, and not really worthy of a Thursday. I’d have no complaints if this had appeared on a Tuesday or Wednesday, but I’m getting a little impatient waiting for a real Thursday entry to come along.

And while I wait, does anyone know where I can go watch some corgi races? Now that looks like fun.

Fun puzzle, although I still had to look several answers up due to them crossing.
21 across kept me combing through the puzzle for a while. At first I thought of a US citizen and put down ESP. Then after EAGLET I though, "OH! Of course, it's ASL! I don't think anyone learns ASL just to be bilingual but cool of the constructor to raise awareness"

Had no idea ESL was a widely used acronym but here I am, someone with English as a second language 🙈

I enjoyed the solve but I just have one qualm. I understand completely that the bigger boxes for the rebuses was literally part of the theme and revealer but I always prefer when rebuses or indeed any themed entry is not highlighted and requires a penny dropping moment instead where you realise that the letters you want to use just won’t fit into those boxes. I just find that a Thursday in particular should avoid the handholding. But thanks anyway for the fun puzzle!
This will mean little to anyone but me, but the first time I’ve (knowingly) beaten the xwstats average.

While I understand the theme, neither Aldi nor Gap are big box stores, at least in the US where big box stores are the IKEAs, Best Buys, and Home Depots of the world.

Aldi is a regular supermarket (and in fact smaller than a traditional US supermarket) and Gap is a standard clothing store. Nothing big box about either of them. 

I also agree that this was more Tuesday or Wednesday material given that, due to the theme, the rebus boxes were highlighted making it a much easier solve. Enjoyable nonetheless.
I enjoy rebuses (rebii anyone?) and this was a fun puzzle to boot. Too easy for a Thursday (24: 14 better than my avg. Thursday) but I’ll take the win and the music!

I get it.  You kind of need the boxes for the theme to work.  They're not all big box stores, but they are all stores in big boxes.

Very cute, but the boxes are a huge tell, and take this puzzle out of Thursday territory.  As soon as I cued up the puzzle to print, I felt a little let down.

I wonder if Joe was surprised that this ran on Thursday instead of Wednesday.

This would have been my first Thursday without lookups (and four without lookups in a row) except that I gave up and checked to find that I had OLDEST instead of ELDEST for 43A [Most senior]. 

A fun solve with the clever rebuses that worked both ways, but I would agree with others that those stores are not all what we would refer to as BIGBOXSTORES--unless you want to buy ten pairs of blue jeans at the GAP. But that doesn't matter--a little poetic license is okay with me.

By the way, 44D [Nursery purchases] suggests buying SOILS. Any organic gardener knows that you can make your own--with all kinds of stuff: grass clippings, leaves, shrub prunings, spent plants, kitchen scraps, coffee grounds, eggshells, old sod, etc. Dig a pit or make a pile, throw it in, water, give it sun, turn it over--poof! SOIL.

I enjoyed figuring out the rebuses. We just got our first Aldi in Maine and not on my radar, so I had a double rebus there for the longest time, local bar instead of dive across and Vivaldi down (alb/aldi for my rebus). I put lowest in top right, so missed that rebus until I got The Gap.  Phew. Then could go back to fix things, enjoying a couple of aha moments. I also broke down and looked up seraphs, as bottom left gave me the most trouble.  Should have known "strike a pose" from my Madonna listening years!

As former (short-term) GAP employee
( I survived exactly the number of days
to get the big pre-holiday employee discount day)

the worst possible post was as a greeter.

During the holidays.
In a mall.

The non-stop flow of people
and your own voice,
“Hi there!”
“Hello”
“Hi”
“Hello”
“Hellohitherehellohellohihihitherehello…”

Hours.

Almost felt like hazing newer employees.

But *everyone* got Gap clothes for Christmas.


So,there’s that.

I got my brain-loving work-hard moment trying to figure out the LOWES square.

Then came splendid moments of uncovering words and phrases that hit sweet spots – GRAVEN IMAGE, GRAPPA, LIKE A CHARM, ELICIT, TAG LINES, SERAPHS, CHASM, and SOIREE. That’s a load of lovely.

I appreciated the tightness of the theme – and thus Joe’s skill in coming up with the theme answers he did – when I struggled to come up with other rebus possibilities. 

The only other store besides ULTA (which Joe mentioned in his notes) that hit me was UPS, which does have terrific answer possibilities, such as BACKUPSINGER and COUPSDETAT.

And, you know, it’s pretty silly – and I love silly – having a grid with goofy-looking oversized squares that pun on BIG BOX STORE in the first place.

So, your puzzle, Joe, put me in a great mood and sent me flying into my day.  Thank you!

Well, it's nice to have an actual Thursday puzzle on a Thursday. Sadly, the cluing for this one was once again much too direct, especially since the theme was so obvious. Finished in about 13 minutes, and I'm not a speed solver.

Is the GAP a BIGBOXSTORE? That one seemed very out of place to me. And I'm surprised YOGAPOSES and STRIKEAPOSE were allowed to appear in the same grid.

No GEMS here, but GRAVENIMAGE, MENORAH, GRAPPA and SERAPHS were nice entries.

Not knowing what a BIGBOXSTORE is didn’t help. 

Is a LOWESTIMATE really conservative? I’m guessing it depends on which side of the deal you’re on. 

A good Thursday puzzle.

As smooth and satisfying to me as my first mug of tea in the morning, which sat cooling as I dug into this morning’s puzzle.  Hats off, as always, to the clever creators who conjure and craft these puzzles for our daily enjoyment.
I’d really like fewer brand names in the crosswords.  It feels like advertising.

Pretty much agree with other posters today, for the most part. Let’s get out of the way that the boxes for the rebus squares are a little bit bigger, so that makes them “big box” stores whether or not the actual stores are “big box”—but still it is a bit disconcerting that two of them are really “big box” and two aren’t, and they are fairly random otherwise. My main issue is the same one Andrzej brought up. I get that to make the revealer make sense the rebuses need to be indicated—that’s fine, no way around that. But the VIVALDI clue was pretty unconscionable for a Thursday. It’s a very famous piece and the composer’s most famous work. So if you know it, and many do, you fill it in, fill in the rebus because it’s the only place it can go, and voila the theme is immediately obvious. I don’t think the rest of the theme clues were a problem, but that alone is something I don’t think we would have seen 10 years ago. Why not clue it with something more generic, or some lesser known trivia about the composer?
That said did I enjoy the puzzle otherwise? Sure, not bad, a few more recycled clues than I would like and some obvious ones, but some interesting ones as well. But still getting the theme so quickly takes it from a “wow, what a great theme and how fun” to “ok, that’s done, I look forward to the weekend and maybe next Thursday will be more exciting”.
Loved the puzzle, but gotta say, GAP and ALDI are not BIGBOXSTORES.

What's ALDI, I wondered?  I thought it was either a shoe or an Italian designer.  I wanted to know so I could guess at the other rebuses.  (I love rebuses so much -- especially the ones with a lot of letters.)  So I looked up ALDI.

Turns out ALDI is a supermarket chain.  What other supermarket chains do we have in Manhattan?  A&P.  Gristede's.  This is not helping.

What I'm finding, rebus-wise, are stores that are not supermarkets like IKEA and GAP.  Also LOWES -- a name I've heard but I don't know what it is exactly.  What do these stores have in common, I wonder?  What will be the revealer?  I have no idea whatsoever.

When BIG BOX STORE comes in I'm wondering if there are any of these at all in Manhattan?  We don't use the phrase -- and if they're here, I don't know where they are.  I'm sure there are some in Queens -- Queens is much more like the rest of the country than Manhattan.  I do know that every time a major retailer online has an article of clothing that I need to try on first, I find that that major retailer does not have a store in Manhattan.  Maybe they did once, but not any more.

But enough of my complaining.  A really entertaining puzzle with rebuses that are gorgeously embedded.  Great fun to solve.  Into my running list for Puzzle of the Year it goes.

A real Thursday puzzle!  Hurray!

Good gimmick. Good revealer. 
And great rebi!

That’s my idea of a near perfect Thursday!!!

I don’t have the energy to read all the comments right now, but I will quickly note that it was another good day to have solved with overlays off. It was still pretty easy to figure out the theme with VIV[ALDI], and a lot of the cluing seemed too easy for a Thursday, but at least I didn’t know in advance where the rebuses would occur. And yeah, without seeing the enlarged boxes in the grid, the revealer did not work that well since ALDI and GAP are not “big box stores”.
Beyond fabulous. Fun theme, no dumb puns, accessible pop-cult references ... just a great puzzle. Thank you!
A great Tuesday puzzle.

Re: 22 D

One day, I'd like to see BABY SNAKES clued as [Pint-size racers].

Cute Thursday puzzle, somewhat on the easy side. I got hung up on the SW for a bit, and had to wait on crosses for a couple others, but once the rebuses were figured out, it came together fairly quickly at the end.

Thanks, Joe.

Another Tues/Wed for a Thurs puzzle. But okay. I got the theme rather quickly when I filled in first IKEA, then ALDI.  So then I got held up for a bit, because I thought all the rebuses were going to be international stores with four letters, to appease our increasingly international audience. When I got GAP, I realized that wasn't the case, so that made it a bit easier.

Like almost everyone else, it took a while to be able to get LOEWS to work, because I was still stuck on the international aspect. I thought that maybe GAP had some international locations.

I knew ALDI from a very seedy one not far from me when I lived in NJ. But within the last year they've opened three locations in Las Vegas. People love them. I only loved them for the great prices on European dark chocolate. Sadly, not one of those three are anywhere close to my house. But that's okay, because the one thing that everyone hates about them is the one thing that would keep me away from them: the requirement to put a quarter into a slot to be able to use a shopping cart, returnable when you hook the cart back up when you're finished. I haven't carried cash since 2020, except for an emergency $20 that harks back to my teenage years and my mother's admonition to always have a few spare bucks "In case you get mugged."

Nice Thursday puzzle. Not all that easy for me, of course, but tumbled to the rebus trick early on with VIVALDI (which I actually remembered). And... puzzle find today. A Tuesday from June 19, 2021 by Alan Arbesfeld. One theme clue and answer example: "Toy with a spring, literally" THEJACKBOX And all the other theme clues ended with 'literally'. Those answers: THENIPBUD THEBEWAY THEUPAIR THEPIESKY THEBACKDAY Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=6/29/2021&h=17a See you tomorrow. ....

Here I was staring at _ _ A picturing a sexy black BRA and wondering what a Belgian BRA looks like -- lacy I think -- and what sort of mutant, like the one in Total Recall, needs a double BRA.

Shows you where my mind is I guess.

At one point I thought maybe it was going to be "ALDI", going one way, but "LIDL" for the perpendicular crossing.

"Lowes" just eluded me entirely, which is a little surprising since it's where most of my money has gone over the past thirty years; due to a dishonest contractor gutting my house and then disappeating, I ended up rebuilding a lot of it with my own two hands. 

I spent a ridiculous amount of time trying to figure it out, so once I finally caved and looked it up my last entry turned out to be SLOWEST.
9D was easy because Shadow and Jackie's chicks have been doing great.

GOKARTS...I guess I don't actually know what those look like.  And I've never been in a GAP, though I know it's a STORE.
Then, in these days of so much suspicion, I started 5D with FAKE... and at 57D I expected some AI interference!  

Haven't watched any off "The Pitt" but I knew it was a medical drama, so DOC was expected...  CanNOT keep up with all the TV shows, rappers, singers, movies, and brands!

Despite being "frequent fliers" during our variious rehab and renovation projects (two floods, one tornado, and 3 fixer-uppers) I was very slow to come up with the entry for Square #12.  And Home Depot did get some of our business...

You know, Mississippi doesn't have a single Trader Joe's, and I don't know of an IKEA, either.  Recenty, an ALDI opened in our suburb; we paid one visit, then left.  It's definitely not being run by German management.  

Fun Puzzle, Joe Deeney!
Fun puzzle. I thought I was so smart by filling in GUESStimates in the upper right…that derailed me for a while.
Rebus. Pass.

Fun, quick Thursday! I know whenever a crossword leaves me feeling smart there will be complaints in the comments of it being too easy. Square One brought up memories of the old PBS show, which I've been showing YouTube clips of to my kid. Now I have That's Infinity stuck in my head

Huh, Shamash is not a city in the Pacific Northwest. Took a lot of crosses to get MENORAH. It was nice to see a rebus puzzle again, but VIV[ALDI] gave it away too easily. [LOWES]TIMATES was quite the challenge, though, despite the fact that I shop at that BBS often.
I LOVED this puzzle....so much fun to figure out!
Why the note that this was better in light mode? Seemed the same to me.

Spoiler alert!

Something strange happened to me when solving this puzzle. The rebus in the upper right was the last remaining square I needed to complete the puzzle. I thought it was "GUESS" at first, but obviously that didn't work. I actually was thinking "LOWES" and as soon as I typed the "L" NYT said I solved the puzzle.

So I got credit for the solve, even though I didn't finish the rebus.
Not sure Aldi and Gap are good examples of big box stores.  Clever concept but clunky execution.

Enjoyed the theme and the cute reveal (which was worth the trick square spoiler). Sailed through until op-ed for OBIT in the south center bamboozled me for several minutes (which made for a satisfying "click" and the sound of multiple pennies dropping).

Always get a small rush of joy when I see an interesting new twist in the grid before starting. A kick of endorphins. Sometimes I take a moment just to enjoy the anticipation.

This one was a fast solve for me. I love the Four Seasons. Not crazy about big box stores--enjoyed them more in this grid than when I'm in them.

Does anybody else read the existing comments before weighing in?
It's tiresome to see the same points being made--and answered--again and again and again.
Do better people!
But not on my lawn!
"Lowest estimate "is the opposite of conservative, explaining why project costs so often appear to increase. If the estimate was "conservative," there would not be a surprise.

This was a fun, relatively breezy Thursday. I agree with the general gist of the comments that the Big Boxes in the grid, although necessary for the revealer to make sense, served to make the puzzle too easy, especially in combination with some of the cluing. vivALDI felt like the Mondayest clue of all time. It made the gimmick immediately obvious. I can see the benefit of an easy Thursday now and then, but this might have tilted a little too far in that direction.
Avoiding any spoiler, I note that one of the answers in the special squares *** really does match the special category of the reveal *** *** *****
I enjoyed this one. Love a themed Thursday. I get the box matched the theme, but this is probably one of the fewest times I could've guessed the rebus.

THANK YOU THANK YOU for having mercy on us!!!  Please continue to emphasize "rebus" squares.  For many years, I've generally quit as soon as as I realize that the correct word won't really fit.  BUT if I knew what it involved, I would be game to continue.  <3 <3 <3
Sean nailed it – this is a really good starter for less than seasoned Thursday solvers just getting the hang of rebuses. Joe, I like rebuses a lot and really enjoyed this puzzle – thanks!

I had to work this one a bit but in a good satisfying way.  I thought it a nice puzzle once I re-remembered how to work the rebus option.  (I am more used to cramming in the letters with my ball point pen.)

Today I learned an awful lot more than I needed to about slime mold.  
Fascinating stuff!

To say Aldi and Gap are “big box stores” is quite a stretch. In fact, Aldi is the OPPOSITE of a big box store, and Gap is an apparel retailer…which is not a big box store. 

And POSE appears twice? Since when did NYT start allowing repeat words in answers? POSE and POSES in this context are not different enough to justify allowing duplication in answers. I’d love to hear Will’s justification here. I got STRIKEAPOSE first, and then I got stuck for a bit because I was like, “It has to be YOGAPOSES, but there’s no way that can be right.”

Absolutely nothing against Joe Deeney…I always enjoy his puzzles, and this was very well constructed as always. This was a failure at the Editorial level.

A well-crafted little amuse-bouche.  It was apparent from the get-go that the theme would involve rebuses ("rebusi"?)  Overall the entries were solid, but the cluing was ever-so-gentle -- this would've made a fabulous Tricky Tuesday, if there were such a thing.  Still hoping for that brain-bending Thursday challenge, though.  Maybe next week!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle
a/ o heart! in such a world as this where a brave of birds wild the sky in a tempest of joy you run in the orchards over d/ the rush of angels just a/ to see
weird thing: the last square i filled in was the 11A/12D rebus, and the puzzle solved with me entering only the first letter of the rebus.

My device does NOT allow me to enter more than 1 letter on the rebus no matter what I do. Brand new device on the nyt app. So my puzzle refuses to say it's finished. Not happy. No way is it worth any money to subscribe to these crossword puzzles.

Just for fun, I looked online for more about BIGBOXSTORES.

I found this description. "Big box stores are massive, high-volume retail establishments characterized by their large physical footprint (typically over 50,000 square feet) and simple, warehouse-like architecture. They leverage economies of scale to offer a wide variety of goods at highly discounted prices."

Here is a disambiguator for everyone who finds themselves confused about the puzzle or about big box stores in general.

Big Box stores are large, box-shaped stores, usually located in outlying suburban areas, often in close proximity to other similar stores (in aggregate, these are referred to as Big Box Developments, or something similar). This is in contrast to a shopping mall—where many stores are located in one building—or main-street style shops, which have individual access but are smaller scale. Walmart, Costco, Ikea, BestBuy, Target, HomeDepot, etc. are the prototypical Big Box stores.

Big Box stores are not (necessarily) stores where you buy large things or large numbers of things. The term refers exclusively to the size and shape of the actual building.

The stores listed in the rebus squares, are not all Big Box stores. The gimmick is that they are stores and they are placed in boxes that are slightly bigger than standard crossword boxes. This might have been more straightforward if they had been consistently either all big box stores or all not, but let's assume these were the ones that worked and leave it at that.

The visual pun of the rebus being a store in a larger box would not have worked without the visual emphasis. While it is often nice to be surprised by a gimmick, this one had to be visual or it would not have made sense.

@Alexis

Generally, a big box store is a very large store containing either a wide variety of merchandise, like Walmart or Target, or requiring a membership and a curated selection of items, like Costco or Sam's Club (which is part of Walmart), or containing a wide selection of items in a single area, such as Best Buy (electronics), furniture (Bob's and Raymour & Flanigan), sporting goods stores, (Dick's, Cabela's).  Huge home improvement stores like Home Depot and LOWE'S fall into this category as well.

Typically, these stores are located outside of indoor shopping malls, usually in freestanding locations with a large surrounding parking lot, or in strip malls, where they are the largest stores, and serve as anchors.  In urban areas, these locations are modified to fit in the environment, and the stores, while still large, may be found on the ground floors of multi-purpose buildings.

@Heidi

IKEA has been present in Poland since 1991. In fact, the first Polish IKEA opened less than 1 km from where I lived at the time. Can you believe it was considered a luxury store then? We were in the middle of our great economic transformation, inflation was raging, and living standards were decreasing for many, before finally improving several years later, once market economy and democracy finally began to provide benefits for most people. These days I would never shop at IKEA - much better quality furniture is available elsewhere, especially as Poland is one of Europe's (the world's, maybe?) biggest furniture producers.

We have ALDI stores, too, albeit of the German supermarkets, Lidl is by far the most ubiquitous, and my favorite (also because they stock a lot of Polish produce).

I've never seen a GAP store in Poland, but I remember the name from my formative years - my mom managed to get her hands on a GAP sweatshirt in the late 1980s, when anything Western was rare and much desired.

LOWES gave me most trouble today as I only vaguely recognize it, probably from 5 or 10 years ago, when I browsed imgur daily, and all sorts of American things came up in the memes there. We don't have that brand over here.

@Heidi

I checked and there actually is an ALDI in the building that housed IKEA in 1991!

Also, I remember that building from the 1980. There was some state-owned store there then. Shops were understocked under "communism", but occasionally new stock would be delivered, and huge lines formed then, with people queueing for hours upon hours. There was a whole queueing culture - people organized queueing committees (komitety kolejkowe) that made sure nobody would cut in line (Pan tu nie stał! - You were not standing here! was a signature catchphrase or pre-1989 Poland). My mom once took me to stand in such a queue - having a kid with you meant you could go and take care of some other stuff while the child kept your spot in the line.

So there we were, my mom standing in line, and me playing around on the nearby underground (Metro) construction site. I was throwing stuff into a huge puddle when my foot slipped and I fell into the dirty water. It was winter, and close to freezing. I begged mom to take me home but she couldn't - she had spent hours in line already and she really needed whatever it was that was being sold. By the following day it would be gone. So I stood there, shivering.

Fun times, eh?
